heading: XXXVI SAVERIO CAMPANINI
card_summary: This chunk is an editorial section by Saverio Campanini documenting the autoptic (first-person eyewitness) testimony scattered across Zorzi's various works regarding his pilgrimage to the Holy Land, undertaken around 1494. Campanini traces Zorzi's references to specific sites — the tomb of Mary (dormitio), Rachel's tomb near Bethlehem, the Nativity grotto at Bethlehem, the patriarchs' tombs and the Hospital of Sant'Abramo at Hebron, the oasis of Jericho and the Mount of the Quarantine, the Dead Sea (called "Asfalto" or "Malach"), and Mount Sinai — drawing on parallel passages in the De harmonia mundi, the Problemata, the Commento all'Elegante Poema, and Zorzi's commentary on Giovanni Pico della Mirandola's Conclusiones. Campanini shows how Zorzi integrates direct pilgrimage experience into his kabbalistic and theological speculations, identifying Hebron's charity hospital with Abraham's divine attribute of mercy (hesed/misericordia) and interpreting the Dead Sea's desolation as a permanent sign of divine wrath against Sodom and Gomorrah. The plant known as "pomo di Sodoma" (Calotropis procera) is identified as a real botanical specimen confirming Zorzi's description.
essay:
## Overview

This section (pp. 36–41) is editorial commentary by Saverio Campanini rather than a translated passage from Zorzi's *De harmonia mundi* itself. Campanini systematically catalogues the autoptic testimony — eyewitness statements beginning with phrases like "come abbiamo verificato di persona" or "chi lo ha visto con i propri occhi" — that Zorzi distributed across his corpus to document his ca. 1494 pilgrimage to the Holy Land. The section moves geographically from Jerusalem southward through Bethlehem, Hebron, Jericho, the Dead Sea, and toward Sinai, tracking how personal observation becomes material for Zorzi's kabbalistic and cosmological speculation.

## The Dormitio of Mary and Rachel's Tomb

Campanini opens by noting Zorzi's precise topographic knowledge, which was either based on notes taken during the pilgrimage or on the rich devout travel literature of the period. Two tombs are introduced first. The tomb of Mary in Jerusalem, site of the *dormitio*, is cited from the *Problemata*:

> Dunque, che a Maria sia stato garantito tale privilegio [di essere assunta in cielo] non può essere revocato in dubbio. L'indizio più cogente di ciò è il fatto che la sua tomba sia vuota.

<cite>p. 36, from the Problemata</cite>

The empty tomb is deployed as theological proof for the Assumption. Similarly, Rachel's tomb on the road from Jerusalem to Bethlehem — a standard pilgrimage waypoint — is quoted from the *Commento all'Elegante Poema* in Zorzi's Venetian vernacular, confirming that Zorzi "saw with his own eyes" ("come abbiamo visto con i nostri occhi") the monument near Ephrata.

## Bethlehem and the Grotto of the Nativity

Zorzi's references to Bethlehem are brief but geographically specific. The *Problemata* places Bethlehem south of Jerusalem and describes the Nativity grotto as situated "in aperta campagna" outside the city walls, in a wooded spot where the Magi found the Christ child. Campanini notes these details are consistent with standard medieval pilgrimage itineraries.

## Hebron: Abraham, Hospitality, and Kabbalistic Misericordia

The most theologically loaded passage in this chunk concerns the Hospital of Sant'Abramo at Hebron, which Zorzi integrates into his kabbalistic topography of divine attributes. Campanini quotes two versions of the same observation — from the *De harmonia mundi* and from Zorzi's commentary on Pico della Mirandola's *Conclusiones* — which differ only in the daily quantity of bread distributed (12,000–13,000 loaves in one version; 5,000 in the other). The *De harmonia mundi* version reads:

> La preoccupazione principale di Abramo era infatti l'ospitalità verso i viaggiatori e altre opere pie. Perciò nella valle sopra ricordata [ovvero Hebron] è stato edificato un luogo di accoglienza, che esiste ancora ai giorni nostri, detto ospedale di Sant'Abramo, nel quale vengono distribuiti ogni giorno dodici o tredicimila pani, e carne o altro companatico in misura corrispondente alla quantità di pane. Lo sappiamo per averlo visto di persona e averne mangiato.

<cite>pp. 37–38, De harmonia mundi</cite>

Campanini emphasizes that Zorzi does not merely cite this as travel curiosity: he connects Abraham's legendary hospitality to the kabbalistic identification of the patriarch with the *sefirah* of *hesed* (mercy/lovingkindness), which in the *qabbalah*'s mystical geography is associated with the south — the same direction in which Hebron lies relative to Jerusalem. The pilgrimage experience thus becomes evidence for, and illustration of, Zorzi's broader project of mapping kabbalistic structure onto the physical world.

## Jericho and the Mount of the Quarantine

Zorzi visited the oasis of Jericho and the mountain above it traditionally identified as the site of Christ's forty-day fast and temptation (*Mons Quarantanae*). He states he personally examined a painting there depicting Jesus's combat with the serpent:

> Su quel monte salì Gesù per digiunare e combattere il serpente antico, secondo quanto si narra e si può apprendere da una pittura che ho esaminato personalmente.

<cite>p. 38, Problemata</cite>

## The Dead Sea: Divine Wrath Fossilized in Landscape

Campanini devotes particular attention to Zorzi's descriptions of the Dead Sea — called *Asfalto* (from the bitumen/asphalt) or *Mare Malach* / *melach* (Hebrew for "salt") — which across multiple works Zorzi treats as a permanent material sign of divine judgment against Sodom and Gomorrah. The *Problemata* passage is the most extensive, cataloguing the sea's poisonous waters, the death of fish entering it, the fruits along its shores that appear genuine but are filled with ash or dust inside, the salt and pitch extracted from its waters, and the birds that fall dead when flying over it:

> Donde deriva il mare detto Malach [melach] ovvero Asfalto e perché le sue acque sono velenose e letali? Non si tratta forse di un lago derivato dalle acque del Giordano nella Pentapoli della Siria, detto comunemente Mar Morto? […] I prodigi di quell'acqua servono a rendere evidente la malvagità di coloro che vi sono stati affogati dal sommo Moderatore travalicando il corso ordinato della natura.

<cite>pp. 38–39, Problemata</cite>

Campanini notes that the detail of the fruits filled with smoke or ash ("pomi di Sodoma") has a long literary genealogy, with Beda the Venerable and Jacopo da Verona as important witnesses. However, he cautions against dismissing Zorzi's first-person testimony: the plant in question, *Calotropis procera*, genuinely exists in desert regions and its hollow, ash-filled pods are a real botanical phenomenon. This is a characteristic Campanini move — recovering the empirical substrate beneath what might look like bookish convention.

A parallel passage from the commentary on Pico's *Conclusiones* (tesi 36, first series) again underlines the eyewitness frame:

> Quale segno dell'ira divina che ancora brucia a danno [degli abitanti di Sodoma e Gomorra], la terra di quel paese è del tutto sterile come cenere, l'acqua è salata, i frutti sono colmi di fumo, gli uccelli che la sorvolano cadono al suolo stecchiti. Chi lo ha visto con i propri occhi rende testimonianza.

<cite>p. 40, Commentary on Pico's Conclusiones, tesi 36</cite>

The *Commento all'Elegante Poema* further reads the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah typologically, comparing it to the universal Flood: the Flood was by water (representing the Son) and the pentapolitan judgment by fire (representing the Holy Spirit), weaving the two events into a Trinitarian schema.

## Mount Sinai: Possible but Uncertain Visit

Campanini closes the section by noting that Zorzi's works contain numerous coherent references to the distance from Jerusalem to Mount Sinai (approximately fifteen days' journey), which may indicate personal knowledge of the route. However, it cannot be confirmed that Zorzi actually reached the monastery of St. Catherine: Pietro Casola's chronicle records that in 1494 the monastery had been raided by infidels who killed the abbot, causing some pilgrims to abandon the Sinai excursion. Zorzi's *De harmonia mundi* comments on Galatians 4:25 (Paul's typological reading of Sinai and Hagar) by noting that Paul's reference to Sinai being "near" Jerusalem is metaphorical — a nearness of influence (*vicinanza dell'influsso*), not spatial proximity — and draws an analogy to Psalm 133's image of the dew of Hermon descending on Mount Zion, though the two mountains are some eighty miles apart.

## Technical Terms and Broader Significance

Key technical terms introduced or used in this section include: *melach* (Hebrew for "salt," applied to the Dead Sea), *hesed* (kabbalistic attribute of mercy associated with Abraham and the south), *qabbalah* (mystical topology of divine attributes), *dormitio* (tradition of Mary's death before Assumption), and *Pentapoli* (the five cities of the plain). Campanini's editorial method throughout is to establish Zorzi as a reliable empirical witness whose direct experience of the Holy Land gives concrete grounding to the kabbalistic-cosmological system elaborated in the *De harmonia mundi* and companion works. The pilgrimage is not incidental biography but epistemological foundation: Zorzi's autoptic testimony authorizes him to read landscape as divine text.
